items.evidence_text:
Client 1: Around one-sixty, give or take.
Client 2: It’s a little higher than that—closer to one-sixty-seven a year gross.
Advisor: Okay, about $167,050 gross household income annually. Thanks—hang on, I’m just writing that down.
Advisor: And just to make sure I’m not mixing this up—Mary, your piece is the self-employed income, right?
Client 1: Yes, I’m self-employed.

Advisor: Great. Going back to what you said earlier, Mary, about not getting caught short—let’s talk monthly spending. What do you two spend per month, all-in, not counting debt payments?
Client 1: Like three grand a month.
Client 2: It’s more like $3,200. Three is too low.
Advisor: Okay—$3,200 per month in expenses. When you say “not counting debt,” you mean like the loan payment is separate, right?
Client 1: Yeah—although I always mix that up. I kind of think of it all as bills.

Client 2: And I’m retired, but I still have some income lines coming in.
Advisor: When you say “income lines,” is that like bonuses and business income still hitting for you, Michae?
Client 2: Exactly. There’s an annual bonus—about $32,050. Then a monthly business income stream that annualizes to about $22,700. And a quarterly bonus that’s about $112,300 annualized.
Client 1: Wait—those bonuses are mine though, not his.
Client 2: No—sorry, Mary—on the statements they’re in my name. You’re thinking of your client work.

Advisor: Perfect. Mary, you mentioned earlier you don’t want to slow down—so let’s look at investable assets next. Ballpark, what do you have across investments and cash-like accounts, excluding the house?
Client 1: I mean—half a million-ish?
Client 2: It’s closer to $580,450 total investable assets.
Advisor: Okay, $580,450 investable assets total. Thanks. Let’s break that down so I’m not guessing—what are the main accounts?
Client 2: There’s a joint taxable account on a retirement platform—about $346,550.

Client 2: Yes, private markets—about $22,250, held at a bank.
Advisor: Got it—$22,250 in private markets at a bank. And then the house—just to acknowledge it even though it’s not investable—what do you estimate the primary residence value at?
Client 1: About $241,200.
Advisor: Okay, $241,200 for the primary residence. Thanks.
Advisor: Now, protection—do you have any disability or similar coverage in place, Mary?

Advisor: One loan at $300 monthly. What kind of loan is it—car, personal, something else?
Client 1: It’s basically a small loan—we used it for some equipment.
Client 2: And the balance is about $2,600 outstanding.
Advisor: Got it—about $2,600 left. Do you know the interest rate on that loan?
Client 2: Yes—9.14%.

Client 1: Around one-sixty, give or take.
Client 2: It’s a little higher than that—closer to one-sixty-seven a year gross.
Advisor: Okay, about $167,050 gross household income annually. Thanks—hang on, I’m just writing that down.
Advisor: And just to make sure I’m not mixing this up—Mary, your piece is the self-employed income, right?
Client 1: Yes, I’m self-employed.

Advisor: One loan at $300 monthly. What kind of loan is it—car, personal, something else?
Client 1: It’s basically a small loan—we used it for some equipment.
Client 2: And the balance is about $2,600 outstanding.
Advisor: Got it—about $2,600 left. Do you know the interest rate on that loan?
Client 2: Yes—9.14%.

Advisor: Hi Mary, hi Michae—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we dive in, how are you two doing today?
Client 1: Doing okay—busy, honestly. I’ve got like an hour, so I’m hoping we can keep it moving.
Client 2: Yeah, same. I’m fine—just want to make sure we’re accurate.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it tight. Mary, tell me a little about what prompted you to set this up—what are you hoping we nail down?

Client 1: Yeah—sounds good.
Advisor: Okay—thanks. Going back to that cash bucket you mentioned earlier, Mary, we’ll circle back to how much you want sitting there. But first I want to lock in a couple household basics so I don’t assume wrong.
Advisor: You two are married, right—married or civil partners?
Client 1: Married, yes.
Client 2: Yep, married.

Client 1: No kids.
Client 2: None—zero dependents.
Advisor: Got it—zero. Mary, earlier you were saying you’re comfortable taking risk if it moves the needle. If you had to pick a label—conservative, moderate, aggressive—where do you land as a household?
Client 1: Aggressive. I mean—within reason, but yeah, aggressive.
Client 2: I’d say aggressive too, just… intentional about it.

Client 1: Around one-sixty, give or take.
Client 2: It’s a little higher than that—closer to one-sixty-seven a year gross.
Advisor: Okay, about $167,050 gross household income annually. Thanks—hang on, I’m just writing that down.
Advisor: And just to make sure I’m not mixing this up—Mary, your piece is the self-employed income, right?
Client 1: Yes, I’m self-employed.

Advisor: Okay, so “other state,” got it.
Client 2: Right—call it Other.
Advisor: Thanks. And then—tax bracket. Earlier you mentioned being in a higher bracket sometimes. Are we talking roughly the 35% bracket?
Client 1: I thought it was 32%.
Client 2: No—hang on—federal, we’ve been at 35% lately.

Advisor: Hi Mary, hi Michae—good to meet you both. I’m Alex. Before we dive in, how are you two doing today?
Client 1: Doing okay—busy, honestly. I’ve got like an hour, so I’m hoping we can keep it moving.
Client 2: Yeah, same. I’m fine—just want to make sure we’re accurate.
Advisor: Totally hear you. We’ll keep it tight. Mary, tell me a little about what prompted you to set this up—what are you hoping we nail down?
Client 1: Big picture: I want a plan that doesn’t slow us down. Income’s lumpy, and I don’t want to get caught short on cash.

Client 2: No—hang on—federal, we’ve been at 35% lately.
Advisor: Got it—thanks for the correction. So we’ll use 35% as the bracket band for planning.
Advisor: Quick admin thing: the way my firm is set up, we’re a US RIA—so you’re working with a US registered investment advisor. Does “US RIA” sound right for how you want this categorized?
Client 1: Sorry—RIA like, that’s not the brokerage account, right?
Advisor: Good question—no, not an account type. It’s just the advisor channel—registered investment advisor in the US.

Advisor: Joint taxable around $346,550—got it. Mary, you also have a retirement account—401(k) or IRA—what’s that sitting at?
Client 1: About $140,900. It’s at a bank—well, the custodian is a bank.
Advisor: Great—Mary retirement account about $140,900 at a bank. Any other cash or bank-type assets?
Client 2: There’s a joint bank-type account held through an insurance provider—about $70,750.
Client 1: That’s the one I call “the cash bucket.”

Client 2: No—sorry, Mary—on the statements they’re in my name. You’re thinking of your client work.
Advisor: Okay, good catch—so those three income lines are under you, Michae. Mary, what’s your gross annual from self-employment, roughly?
Client 1: About $131,300 gross.
Advisor: And Michae, your gross annual income number we’re using is about $35,750, even though it’s made up of those lines—does that sound right?
Client 2: Yes—$35,750 gross is what we’ve been using as my total.

Client 2: Yes—Mary is Client 1, primary. I’m Client 2.
Client 1: Yep, that’s right.
Advisor: Perfect—thanks. Now, Mary, I want to make sure I’m describing what you do correctly. What would you call your occupation group—like broadly—sales, marketing, operations, that kind of bucket?
Client 1: Sales. I mean, it’s my business, but it’s basically sales.
Advisor: Okay, sales—thank you. And you’re self-employed, as we said earlier.

Client 1: Uh, June 7th, 1982.

Client 2: It’s about $35,750 gross for me.
